author: nero_eth
content:
Regarding the bitlist, I'd just drop it. Builders can lie about it without any economic consequences, and proposers wouldn't even care as long as they get their unconditional payment. Independent from the bitlist, builders who produce invalid payloads (bc of censoring) will lose reputation and should only ever be connected to with unconditional payments.
So if it's simpler and nothing really changes, drop it. One can still have the bitlist between builders and validators on the p2p.

author: bridge-bot
content: <soispoke> to me it's not really about proposers, more about improving liveness guarantees

author: nero_eth
content: for whom are the bids if not proposers?

author: nero_eth
content: I thought we'd put there to allow the proposer to pick a bid that is a superset of its own IL view

author: bridge-bot
content: <soispoke> It is for proposers but the concern is not about them receiving payments it’s about them choosing bids associated with payload that are invalid which results in liveness failures

author: jih2nn
content:
Let's say that `IL_A` contains a unique and valid transaction among ILs, and a payload is not full. It can improve liveness compared to the case without bitlist when:

1) The highest bidder couldn't listen to `IL_A`.
2) A proposer listened to `IL_A`.
3) The majority of attesters listened to `IL_A`.

Because without bitlist, the payload should've been reorged. Note that this requires honestly set bitlists, which might not be true in trusted payment.

author: nero_eth
content: right but economically rational proposer wouldn't care as long as they are paid. And honest proposers would disconnect from builders that have caused invalid payloads (not obeying the IL) in the past

author: 0xunclebill
content: it would be irrational to lie about the bitlist from the builders pov

author: 0xunclebill
content: why not just avoid the invalid payload in the first place

author: nero_eth
content: why wouldn't a builder (ignoring reputation) not just always set all bits to 1?

author: 0xunclebill
content: that seems irrational to me, the payload is invalid and they lose money

author: nero_eth
content: So the bits will be enforced?

author: nero_eth
content: Instead of just being for signaling?

author: jih2nn
content: Yes it will be part of forkchoice.

author: 0xunclebill
content: i would assume a proposer wouldnt even accept a bid unless all bits set to 1. probably should just drop those bids on p2p as well maybe

author: nero_eth
content: Ah I see, so not just signaling. And if the builder claims to have set a bit too much, e.g. one where the IL was never available?

author: 0xunclebill
content: yeah maybe all bits set to 1 is aggressive, probably something like bits set match the proposers view

author: jih2nn
content: It will be basically no-op. If their payload turns out to be not IL-compliant, clients maybe downscore or disconnect the builder.

author: jih2nn
content: I think it's helpful to examine trustless and trusted payment cases separately.

author: jih2nn
content:
Here is another example that the bitlist can prevent an honest builder from being forced to pay when they couldn't listen to some IL.

1) The highest bidder couldn't listen to `IL_A`.
2) The highest bidder used trustless payment.
3) A proposer couldn't listened to `IL_A`.
4) The majority of attesters listened to `IL_A`.

This can be useful when network is not good, software bug, etc.

author: nero_eth
content: yeah agree, although, in the trusted part you can always be screwed by having the builder not release the payload. So, maybe we can even ignore it as it's in control of builders/relays anyway

author: jih2nn
content:
Exactly. For trusted payment, builders can lie about the bitlist because they won't pay anyway once their payload is reorged. A proposer should be aware of taking a risk of not getting paid when using trusted payment.

Builders might be confident that they are able to get all ILs via good network connection, req/resp, etc, and just try to compete with bid value. However, this might reveal their private transactions and I'm not sure what would be their preferences/strategies.

author: nero_eth
content: and for the trustless payment, the bit list allows the proposer to contribute to liveness of the chain, even though the proposer has no economic incentive for doing so.

author: bridge-bot
content: <soispoke> I think the fact that he has no incentives to « not do so » is another way to think about

author: jih2nn
content: It depends on the design. If the protocol reorgs a block with a subset bitlist, the proposer has economic incentive to do so.

author: nero_eth
content: if the proposer goes for the trustless payment and the highest bid comes with a all 1s bitlist, rational proposers would pick it. Assuming one IL was never published

author: bridge-bot
content: <soispoke> As in, proposers will act honestly in the default case which helps

author: nero_eth
content: right, I'm more thinking of the all 1s bitlist in a scenario where one IL was never published. In this case proposer gets paid, builder is fine, everythings good even though the bitlist was overly optimistic?

author: jih2nn
content: Yes, this is why builders would have no reason to lie their bitlists when using trustless payment.

author: jih2nn
content: So we assume the payload is good, right? Yes, everything will be fine. Because you cannot know whether the IL existed or not in a distributed system.

author: nero_eth
content: Or put differently, builders have no consequences to fear when setting bits optimisticly

author: jih2nn
content: As long as their payload is good.

author: nero_eth
content: E.g. trustless payment, payload is fine, payload obeys majority attesters view but bitlist overly optimistic (e.g. all 1s although 1 IL committee member was offline)

author: nero_eth
content: I see yeah, this might be fine

author: nero_eth
content: Ofc means, builders may just do all 1s all the time

author: jih2nn
content: Note that this also holds for trusted payment.

author: jih2nn
content: My understanding of the benefit of bitlist is it prevents or mitigates the worst case, i.e., payload reorg, an honest builder being forced to pay when acting honest because of their network issue or whatnot, etc.

author: jih2nn
content: It also aligns the proposer's incentive to choose only bids that have IL-compliant payloads, although it's their best attempt (because they will never know the payload's IL compliance until it's revealed.)

author: nero_eth
content:
Hmm I'm not sure if this holds - like, being a defense for builders.
As a honest/economically rational validator, I will always pick the highest unconditional payment, no matter what's in the bitlist

author: jih2nn
content: This holds for both trustless and trusted payments, if the protocol reorgs bids with subset bitlists.

author: jih2nn
content: Yeah, but what if the protocol reorgs your block if it has a subset bitlist?

author: jih2nn
content: Here, it's a beacon block, not a payload.

author: nero_eth
content: right yeah, I'd pick the highest bit that is at the same time at least a superset of my local view

author: jih2nn
content: And this is what I meant by incentive alignment for proposers.

author: nero_eth
content: gotcha, now it makes sense.

author: jih2nn
content: Thanks for the discussion, I'm sure it'd be helpful for other people as well.
